key_insight: A Bayesian deconvolution model can separate true treatment-related biomarker effects from natural biomarker clearance in early-phase, non-controlled trials.
clinical_relevance: This could help researchers infer whether a therapy is working, estimate dose response, and design smaller prospective trials for acute organ injury studies.
method: Fits individual biomarker trajectories with an exponentially modified Gaussian model.
Separates injury kinetics from biomarker clearance using a historic cohort as the null reference.
Uses simulation and published case-series data to test dose detection and pharmacodynamic signatures.
evidence_signal: In simulation, the framework lowered the minimum detectable treatment effect from 67.5% to 24.5% and was then applied to published case-series data to recover signatures consistent with pre-clinical findings.
watch_item: Performance depends on good historical control data and indication-specific recalibration, so results may not transfer reliably without local validation.
paper_title: Efficacy inference in early-phase non-controlled clinical trials via Bayesian biomarker deconvolution
